---
title: "Business Understanding"
author: "Shawn Fisher"
date: "07/16/2026"
output: html_document
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

## Purpose

This project applies demand forecasting methods to the Rossmann Store Sales
dataset, a historical record of daily sales across ~1,100 drugstore locations
in Germany. The goal is to build, evaluate, and document a forecasting
workflow that mirrors what a retail analytics team would actually use to
plan inventory and staffing — not just to produce an accurate model, but to
show the reasoning, assumptions, and business context behind it. This
mirrors the kind of forecasting work I do professionally, applied here to a
public dataset so the process and results can be shared openly.

## The Business Problem

Rossmann store managers currently forecast sales up to six weeks in advance
using local knowledge and judgment, and forecast accuracy varies widely
store to store. In a real retail operation, a demand forecast like this
directly drives decisions such as:

- How much inventory to order and when, to avoid both stockouts and excess
  holding costs
- How to staff stores for anticipated foot traffic
- Where and when to run promotions, and what lift to expect from them
- Regional or corporate planning around expected revenue

The core business problem this project addresses is: **can a systematic,
data-driven forecast outperform ad hoc, store-level judgment, and by how
much?**

## Who Uses This Forecast

In a real organization, a forecast like this would have several different
audiences, each needing something different from it — this maps closely
onto the audience structure I already work with as a workforce management
(WFM) forecaster, just applied to inventory and staffing instead of contact
volume:

- **Store-level operations** care about short-term, granular forecasts (daily,
  per store) to guide staffing and local ordering decisions — the retail
  equivalent of an interval-level staffing forecast in a contact center.
  Getting the *shape* of demand right (which days are busy, not just the
  average) matters as much as getting the total right.
- **Regional or supply chain planning** cares more about aggregated,
  medium-term forecasts (weekly, across store clusters) to plan
  replenishment and distribution — comparable to capacity planning at the
  site or network level in WFM, where the question shifts from "how many
  people/units today" to "are we structurally over- or under-resourced this
  quarter."
- **Finance and executive stakeholders** care about higher-level, longer-horizon
  trends — is the forecast suggesting revenue is tracking to plan, and where
  is the biggest risk of a miss? This is the same role long-range volume
  forecasts play in headcount and budget planning conversations.

A single forecasting approach rarely serves all three well, which is worth
keeping in mind when evaluating results: a model that's great at the
aggregate level might still be too noisy to be useful store-by-store, and
vice versa. This is a familiar tension from WFM — an interval forecast
tuned for daily staffing decisions is not the same model you'd want feeding
a quarterly budget conversation, even though both are "the same" forecast
at different resolutions.

## What "Good" Looks Like

For this project, I'll primarily track **MAPE** (mean absolute percentage
error) since it's intuitive to communicate to non-technical stakeholders,
supplemented by **bias** (whether the model systematically over- or
under-forecasts). Consistent bias is often more operationally costly than
random error — a model that's always 5% high is easier to plan around than
one that swings unpredictably between +15% and -15%. This mirrors how I
evaluate forecasts in WFM: a persistent understaffing bias erodes service
level in a very different way than a volatile but on-average-correct
forecast, even if both post similar aggregate error.

I'll also watch **WAPE** (weighted absolute percentage error) at the
aggregate level, since it weights errors by sales volume and avoids letting
low-volume stores or days distort the headline accuracy number the way
plain MAPE can — the same reason volume-weighted accuracy is preferred over
simple averages when reporting forecast performance across queues or sites
of very different sizes.

Beyond the headline numbers, I want to evaluate accuracy and bias
*conditionally* rather than just in aggregate — split out by day of week,
promo vs. non-promo days, and holiday periods. A model can be accurate on
average while being consistently wrong on the days that matter most
operationally (weekends, promotions), the same way an interval forecast can
look fine in a daily rollup while still missing peak-hour staffing needs.
This kind of conditional accuracy check is standard practice in WFM and is
often the difference between a forecast that looks good in a report and one
that's actually trustworthy to plan against.

## Cost of Being Wrong

Forecast errors in retail are asymmetric, and it's worth naming that
explicitly rather than treating all error as equally bad:

- **Underforecasting** risks stockouts — lost sales, and potentially lost
  customer loyalty if it happens repeatedly at the same store.
- **Overforecasting** risks excess inventory — holding costs, markdowns to
  clear stock, and in the case of perishable or seasonal goods, outright
  waste.

Which direction is worse depends on the product and context (a stockout on
a high-margin item is costlier than one on a low-margin item, for example),
but in general retailers tend to weight stockouts as more costly than
modest overstock, since lost sales are gone permanently while excess
inventory can usually be discounted and recovered in part. This project
will treat underforecasting as the higher-cost error, though this
assumption is worth revisiting once real cost data is available.

## Constraints and Assumptions

- This is historical data, not a live feed — there's no ability to see how
  forecasts would perform in a true out-of-sample, real-time setting.
- The dataset doesn't include actual cost data (holding costs, stockout
  costs, margins), so business-impact estimates in later sections will be
  illustrative rather than precise.
- Some stores have missing data or closures that need to be handled
  explicitly rather than silently dropped or imputed without comment.
- The scope here is sales forecasting only — this project won't model
  downstream decisions like actual inventory ordering or staffing
  optimization, though it's framed with those use cases in mind.

## Next Steps

The next step is exploratory data analysis: understanding the shape of the
data, checking for missingness and outliers, and looking for the seasonal
and promotional patterns that will inform which forecasting approach makes
sense. Open questions going in: how much do individual stores vary from
each other, and does a single global model make sense, or will store-level
or cluster-level models perform meaningfully better?
